summary: Markets surged to fresh records Wednesday as President Trump indefinitely extended the Iran ceasefire, removing a major geopolitical overhang and igniting a powerful risk-on rally. The Nasdaq Composite jumped 1.6% to a new all-time high, led by semiconductor stocks on their 16th consecutive day of gains—the longest winning streak ever. The S&P 500 added nearly 1% to notch its own record close while the Dow climbed 0.6%. Bitcoin rallied above $78,000 and Ethereum pushed toward $2,400 as crypto markets tracked the relief trade. Tesla beat earnings estimates after the bell, lifting shares 3% in extended trading. Today's focus shifts to a heavy slate of earnings reports including Intel, American Express, Honeywell, and Comcast, as investors assess whether the peace-driven rally has legs ahead of next week's FOMC meeting on April 29.

signals.title: Trump extends Iran ceasefire indefinitely, stocks hit all-time highs
signals.desc: President Trump's extension of the ceasefire with Iran removed geopolitical risk premium, sending the S&P 500 and Nasdaq to fresh records and triggering broad risk-on sentiment across equities and crypto.

signals.title: Semiconductors extend historic 16-day winning streak
signals.desc: Chipmakers rallied for a 16th consecutive session—the longest-ever winning streak—fueled by data-center AI demand and ceasefire optimism, with the group outperforming all other sectors.

signals.title: Tesla beats Q1 earnings, stock jumps 3% after hours
signals.desc: Tesla reported Q1 revenue of $22.39B vs. $22.08B consensus and EPS of $0.41 vs. $0.35 expected, sending shares up 3% in after-hours trading and signaling resilience despite production headwinds.

signals.title: Bitcoin tests $78K as short-squeeze risks mount
signals.desc: Bitcoin rallied above $78,000 following the ceasefire extension and strong ETF inflows, with $180M in short liquidations at stake and momentum building for a breakout above key on-chain resistance levels.

signals.title: Fed expected to hold rates at April 29 meeting
signals.desc: Markets price in 99.4% probability the Fed keeps rates at 3.5%-3.75% next week as policymakers balance elevated inflation from the Iran conflict against a softening labor market, with one cut still penciled in for later in 2026.

verdict: Risk appetite is roaring back on Middle East peace hopes, but heavy earnings and next week's Fed meeting will test whether this rally can hold at fresh all-time highs.
